steps.title: Find the greatest common factor
steps.content:
Identify the greatest common factor (GCF) of $9x^6$ and $63x^2$. The GCF of 9 and 63 is 9, and the GCF of $x^6$ and $x^2$ is $x^2$, so the overall GCF is $9x^2$.

Result: GCF = $9x^2$

steps.title: Factor the expression
steps.content:
Factor $9x^2$ out of the expression: $9x^6 - 63x^2 = 9x^2 \cdot x^4 - 9x^2 \cdot 7 = 9x^2(x^4 - 7)$.

Result: $9x^2(x^4-7)$

steps.title: Check by expanding
steps.content:
Verify by expanding: $9x^2 \cdot x^4 = 9x^6$ and $9x^2 \cdot 7 = 63x^2$, so $9x^2(x^4-7) = 9x^6 - 63x^2$. Confirmed.

Result: Factored form is correct

steps.title: Compare with the choices
steps.content:
• "$\left(9\right)(x^3 - 7x^2)^2$": Expanding gives $\left(9\right)(x^6 - 14x^5 + 49x^4)$, which introduces $x^5$ and $x^4$ terms not in the original. Eliminated.
  • "$x^2(9x - 7)^4$": Raising $(9x-7)$ to the fourth power yields a polynomial with five terms; multiplied by $x^2$ this cannot simplify to just two terms. Eliminated.
  • "$9x^2(x - 7)^4$": Expanding $(x-7)^4$ gives $x^4 - 28x^3 + 294x^2 - 1372x + 2401$; after multiplying by $9x^2$ there are far more terms than the original expression. Eliminated.
  • "$9x^2(x^4 - 7)$": Matches exactly. That matches the correct answer — D is correct.


Result: D
